My only concern is that this is a breaking change which probably warrants a deprecation cycle: users who depended on providing `2n+1` args (with the last arg treated as the "default"), will now be met with a TypeError due to a missing `default` kwarg.

I'm thinking if a better solution here is to (1) make `default` default to some value like `"__pyjanitor_default__"` (it can't be None since I suppose None is a valid default value), (2) introduce a DeprecationWarning to when we detect `default` is the special value from step1. Point the user towards using the explicit `default` kwarg. while manually popping the last arg into `default`. (3) Perform the remaining checks as if the user had passed in `default`, i.e., if `len(args)` is odd, then raise error.

As we figured out from offline discussion, the custom implementation of the base64 algorithm was introduced in order to ensure that the encoded string will only contain alpha-num characters (excluding '+', '/' and '=' symbols that may appear in the strings encoded by 'base64' python's library), which may be useful if a user wants to interact with HDK via its SQL interface (that only supports alpha-num col names).

Well, I have a few things to disagree with here:
1. If we really want this baseX encoding then we may use 'base32' version from python's standard library. The output alphabet for the 32 version is exactly alpha-num characters, its disadvantage though is that [the result is approximately ~20% longer than with base64](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Base32#Disadvantages), but I think we could put up with it.
2. As far as I understand the only reason for this special strings encoding is that if a column name originally contained an '_S' substring then it breaks the decoding as we reserved the '_S' string as a separator of the tuple values:
    ```python
    >>> multiindex_col_name = ("level1", "level2")
    >>> encode_without_base64(multiindex_col_name)
    "_T_Slevel1_Slevel2"
    >>> decode_col_name("_T_Slevel1_Slevel2")
    ("level1", "level2")
    >>> decode_col_name(encode_without_base64(("lev_Sel1", "level2")))
    ("lev", "el1", "level2")
    ```
    So for me, it feels like an overkill to use baseX encoding algorithm for every string because of that. One of the reasons I'm against it is that such kind of mangling may make debugging really hard as you just wouldn't understand what the column name is right after you imported pandas df to modin:
    ```python
    >>> base64.b32encode(bytes("simple column name", "utf-8"))
    b'ONUW24DMMUQGG33MOVWW4IDOMFWWK==='
    ```
    Maybe we can use some really special separator to split the tuple values in the encoded column names so there would be less chance we met it in a user's column name and then just raise an error if this special character is actually met (just reserve certain symbols and forbid them in column names).

@maukoquiroga : aggregate and calculation depth are orthogonal.
Depth is about showing the value of calculation when you have variable A depending on B depending on C etc.
You may wwant to stop at B (`max-depth = 2`).

Aggregate is about showing `mean`/`max`/`min` of the variables A or B o C on your whole population.

We could set an env var for the initial invocation, but the issue at hand is the pile of invocations that are made by Pex in the plugin bootstrapping process (i.e. to run `venv` and `pip` itself), so we'd need to ensure that that specific environment variable is propagated. And we'd still need to look at `argv` to figure out which bootstrapping function to run.

It's worth understanding why we use `sys.executable` in the pre-pyoxidizer case: it's to make sure that plugins are configured with the same Python version and platform as being used to run Pants. We could set ICs and platform constraints by inspecting various things under `sys`, but in an environment where a Python interpreter is present, realistically, it should just end up finding the value of `sys.executable`, just with a giant pile more indirection.

Remember that the goal of this is to support running Pants without the need for a Python interpreter to be present. If we didn't use `sys.executable` and relied on the Python support elsewhere in Pants to load Pex:

* we'd need to have a real Python present
* it would need to be the same version as provided with PyOxidizer, 
* and it'd only be necessary for the purpose of downloading the plugins.
* The external interpreter would never actually run the downloaded packages -- those would be loaded inside Pants

Basically, it would entirely defeat the purpose of not needing a Python interpreter

A better solution would be to not fetch plugins by pip at all, and then as long as we can unzip a wheel file into a reliable location, we wouldn't need to invoke Pex and venv at all. I'd be willing to talk about this use case for a future version of Pants; I think it might make a bit of sense.

> That is why I am suggesting the stacks to validate the src match table instead of the SPINEL doing this.

`RadioSpinel` is not really validating the src match table.

Thread stack already validates and manages src match table:
- It detects when table is full when `otPlatRadioAddSrcMatchEntry()` function return `error`.
- It then disables src match table and enables it back when we can fit all entries back in the table.
- It keeps track of which `Child` entries are not yet updated/added in `SrcMatchTable`
(these are all implemented in `SrcMatchTable` and `Child` classes)

 This `assert` in `RadioSpinel` is ensuring its own configuration is correct, so that it can function correctly.
- `RadioSpinel` for "RCP restoration" feature (and only when this feature is enabled) keeps a copy of src match table so it can restore it on RCP (if it resets).
- The `asssert `in `RadioSpinel` is to make sure the local table size in `RadioSpinel` is larger enough.
  - In `RadioSpinel` (and/or OT core) we don't really know how many entries radio/RCP supports, so instead it assumes `MAX_CHILDREN` which it knows is the max Thread stack host will use.
  
My concern with removing the `assert` (or changing it to `VerifyOrExit`) is that it can actually mask an incorrect configuration. 

------

> Not really, so if a protocol supports 802.15.4 association (like zigbee), all joining devices join using indirect messaging. So lets say a router is joining, it will also claim a source match entry, even though it is not technically a child. So to overcome this we have a big enough source match table on RCP but the stack does not define a big MAX_CHILDREN causing this issue.

This is not clear to me. My understating from your earlier answer is that each protocol will be using its own process and its own `RadioSpinel` instance:
- The `RadioSpinel` used with Thread stack would at most have `MAX_CHILDREN` indirect tx.
- If you use a `RadioSpinel` with another stack (say Zigbee), it can define its own `MAX_CHIDLREN` config for what it uses, any reason this would not work?

This might be a pedantic distinction, but a couple concerns with framing this as model architecture selection:
* I think many people understand the term "model architecture" to mean a learning algorithm _without_ the hyperparameters yet specified. E.g. one might say "we used a two-level CNN architecture, and we set the learning rate to 0.9".
* I suspect that, in many cases, the goal is model selection, not architecture/hyperparameter selection.  E.g. every week you want to train an ML model.  When you do that, you consider a bunch of architectures/hyperparameters.  But the next week, you don't just stick with the architecture/hyperparameters that worked best in the previous week - you again try training a bunch of models with a bunch of architectures/hyperparameters and again use the best one.

It may be easiest to discuss in person, but my initial thoughts are:

> This was the most tempting route but I dissuaded myself by arguing that the graph-dashboard or the graph-app should not be aware of how the URL is formed or which route is used by the loader.

I see this differently.  It makes sense to me that tf-graph-dashboard and tf-graph-app should have more direct control over the data sources used by the graph loader, because they draw on very different data sources, and the current `Router` abstraction is a poor fit for the tf-graph-app data source.  The graph app is used in contexts that are totally unrelated to TensorBoard and don't use the TensorBoard URL structure, and in those contexts it doesn't really make sense for the loader to load from a `pluginRoute` because there is no graph "plugin" at all.  (Additional evidence for this is how the fake Router here returns a constant `pbtxtFileLocation` regardless of the `pluginName` or `route` parameters.)

I see the conceptual job of the graph loader as taking a given dataset, including the data sources (whether blobs or URLs), and implementing just the loading of that data - fetching from the URL if needed, and then parsing the result.  It doesn't seem necessary or really feasible to me for the graph loader to fully encapsulate what data sources it will actually need, because for the tf-graph-app use case you need to explicitly provide the data sources up front anyway - it can't just be a private API between the loader and the plugin backend because there is no plugin backend.

Agreed that in the `hydrate_sources` rule, we could remove this marker type to instead check if a codegen edge exists. 

Where this gets really clunky, though, is when we need to filter out irrelevant targets elsewhere. See https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/9638, where we only want `files()`, `resources()`, and Python targets. 

Soon, we will also want all codegen targets. This will soon be achieved with a simple `if tgt.has_field(CodegenSources)`.

Alternatively, if we remove this marker type, we would need to change the rule to take `UnionMembership` as a param, then get the `CodeGenerator` members and iterate over them to see if there's an edge or not. I'm sure we could factor this up, but even with factoring, it will be more complex than `if tgt.has_field(CodegenSources)`.

> Personally, I think it is a source of coming confusions. If the configuration style is to be appreciated, why to keep programmatic relics? They smell like common quick-and-dirty solutions that shouldn't be part of an official and professional piece of software. Do you want to keep this flavor?

You definitely need some fallback when no config file is present in the search directories. (You _could_ make that impossible by adding the source directory to the bottom of the list and adding the template config to the `package_data` directive. But that's not any less obscure IMO.)

> A future user might wonder what those lines stand for and why they seem to have zero effects.

Unlikely. A user capable of browsing the source code will also notice that it _says right here_ that this is only the fallback position.

> Nevertheless, the information itself is not lost when line-drop is committed, since the repository keeps them in it's history.

There's a huge (functional) difference between information preserved in a history and in comments: History can give you the vertical evolution of a piece of code _when you are already_ prepared to dig into it. Comments on the other hand readily avail themselves for information that is helpful to understand the intention of the present state and usually does not enter the history (for being too low-threshold / specific / contingent).

```python
def log_duration(self, fun, sql, param_list):
    start = time()

        return fun(sql, param_list)
    finally:
        stop = time()
        duration = stop - start
        logger.debug(
            '(%.3f) %s; args=%s', duration, sql, param_list,
            extra={'duration': duration, 'sql': sql, 'params': param_list}
        )
```

What do you mean by:

> but error for all other cases

The only case that `GoogleCredentialsMux()` will return an error is when the credentials are structurally corrupt or not found at all. There's not permissions checking or the like here.

The warning about not being able to create a statefile permalink can be done because it's easy to check if the credentials file is missing both `GoogleAccessID` and `PrivateKey`. Go cloud documents that these are required for `SignedURL()`. The reason to log the warning here at all is because this happens before pulumi runs the update, so a user can see the warning before the preview incase it matters to them. It'll obviously log that the permalink can't be made later on if the call to `SignedURL()` fails.

This mutates the location object, which is a bad idea. I suspect that because of sharing of location objects, this code happens to accidentally pass tests, but in general this wouldn't work because it doesn't rewrite locations used within `Expression` objects, e.g. in `Filter` or `ConstructResult` blocks.

> Because the MARF doesn't need to be responsible for either hashing keys or managing the actual storage of values.

Actually, it does -- the whole point of hashing is to ensure that the keys are evenly distributed, no matter what the schedule of inserts is.  This is particularly important to us, because one attack avenue a network adversary has is to hammer the MARF index by inserting and reading many keys that are close to each other in the key space (since these paths will have the most nodes in them and will thus incur the highest I/O costs to service).  Even if they are suitably paid for, it still slows down the peer.  At some point, I was going to audit the code to ensure that the insert/get paths to include the VRF seed hash in order to ensure that keys are evenly distributed, no matter how the contract data are structured.

> The borrow checker gets pretty unhappy if every function requires mutable pointers (which would be the case for the Clarity VM to use .get)

The only solution I can think of is to refactor `get` to open/close a file handle internally as a stack variable.  We'd want to think very carefully about this because it can lead to a resource exhaustion attack -- i.e. we'll want to limit the number of concurrent calls to `.get()` globally.

This is a good topic, and I want to have a discussion for this. 
Currently, MXNet doesn't have its own type system. It's simply using Numpy.dtype. Numpy doesn't natively support bfloat16, so we define bfloat16 as a numpy customized type.
Pros: compatible with current design, isinstance(bfloat16, np.dtype) could return True.
cons: bfloat16.name doesn't work, have to use bfloat16.names[0] instead.
Another solution is, creating MXNet's own data type system, just like pytorch and tf. This is a big API change, so we wish this can be done when upgrading to MXNet 2.0.

Currently, we prefer this approach to enable bfloat16 in MXNet 1.x, and refactor it in MXNet 2.0.

Yes, I believe that those variables currently have wrong values. 

As an example from the PDF reference: 

> For example, assuming revision 2 of the security handler, the value -44 permits printing and copying but disallows modifying the contents and annotations.

With the current code, this is directly interpreted as a bit string (see below). The third bit (printing) is correctly one, but the fifth bit (copying) is wrongly zero, and the fourth bit (modifying) is wrongly one. 

```
>>> '{:032b}'.format(-44)
'-0000000000000000000000000101100'
```

With your new code, this creates another bit string (see below). 

```
>>> p = -44
>>> unsigned_p = p if p > 0 else p + (1 << 32)
>>> '{:032b}'.format(unsigned_p)
'11111111111111111111111111010100'
```

With this new code, the third bit (printing) is correctly one, the fifth bit (copying) is correctly one, and the fourth bit (modifying) is correctly zero. 

Also note that all the higher-order bits are one with the new code. This is also an improvement. 

My conclusion: your code should be used to interpret the value of `P`.

I've figured out a way to get rid of the doctree cache and it seems to be working with my documentation suite. It has these points:

1. Use a `SimpleNamespace` from the `types` standard library:
```python
from types import SimpleNamespace
```

2. Create a "shadow document" object in the Domain who's only purpose is to hold a LoggingReporter that can be passed around:
```python
# if from docname not in _doctree_cache:
#   _doctree_cache[fromdocname] = env.get_doctree(fromdocname)
# doctree = _doctree_cache[fromdocname]

# resnode = role.result_nodes(doctree, env, node, None)[0][0]

shadow_doc = SimpleNamespace(
    reporter=LoggingReporter(env.doc2path(fromdocname)))
resnode = role.result_nodes(shadow_doc, env, node, None)[0][0]

...
     # reporter = doctree.reporter
     # reporter.warning('Cannot resolve reference to %r' % text,
     shadow_doc.reporter.warning('Cannot resolve reference to %r' % text,
```

3. Ensure that HTTPXRefRole implements `result_nodes` to ensure that getting a shadow_document is not going to get up into its super():
```python
def result_nodes(self, document, env, node is_ref):
   return [node], []
```

This copies what its super() currently does, but puts a firewall in place in case that changes.

This lightens the load on getting ahold of the reporter for a document, without having to load in its entire doctree.

`IMapIterator._submit_next_chunk` now indicates when the final chunk is submitted, using `self._result_thread.add_object_ref(new_chunk_id, final=self._finished_iterating)`.

I don't think your cardStepIndex tests have to care about either of these edge cases ( "step is 0", "step is min") - it doesn't really have anything to do with cardStepIndex. 

This is my methodology:

I start by looking at the code like I'm a code coverage tool: I look at all the code in the reducer and make sure that each line of code is covered by at least one test. I make sure that each branch/decision (if statements evaluating true and false, trinary operators evaluating true and false, for loops for multiple elements, etc...) is covered by a test.

When I do that, these are the test cases I see:

* selectedTime is not reinitialized if it doesn't have to be
* selectedTime is initialized with start step 0 when appropriate
* selectedTime is initialized with start step min when appropriate
* we call generateNextCardStepIndexFromSelectedTime when selectTimeEnabled and do something with the result
* we don't call generateNextCardStepIndexFromSelectedTime when !selectTimeEnabled (and so you effectively ignore the result it would return if you did call it)

It's not a perfect mechanism but it usually identifies 80 or 90% of the tests you want to write. To supplement, I also look for places where there might be risk of subtle bugs: Using the wrong variable in place of another, for example. When I do that, this is what I see:

* we pass the new  selectedTime value to generateNextCardStepIndexFromSelectedTime instead of the old null one (nextSelectedTime instead of state.selectedTime). 

What I don't see when I do this sort of analysis is:
* cardStepIndex cares about whether selectedTime.start.step = 0
* cardStepIndex cares about whether selectedTime.start.min = 0

Both those cases are just side-effects of initializing selectedTime and have nothing to do with cardStepIndex in my mind.

To think about it yet another way: If you modified the lines that initialize selectedTime step to 0 or min, do you have at least one test that fails? If you already have one, do you really need another?

Test numerical values:
```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np
from numpy.testing import assert_allclose

def softmaxcrossentropy(x, target, weight=None, reduction='mean'):  # type: (np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray
    max_x = np.max(x, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    exp_x = np.exp(x - max_x)
    p = exp_x / np.sum(exp_x, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    inp = np.log(p)
    input_shape = inp.shape
    if len(input_shape) == 2:
        N, C = input_shape
        neg_gather_element_input = np.zeros((N, ), dtype=np.float32)
        for i in range(N):
            neg_gather_element_input[i] = -inp[i][target[i]]
    if len(input_shape) == 3:
        N, C, D = input_shape
        neg_gather_element_input = np.zeros((N, D), dtype=np.float32)
        for i in range(N):
            for d in range(D):
                neg_gather_element_input[i][d] = -inp[i][target[i][d]][d]

    loss = neg_gather_element_input
    if weight is not None:
        gather_weight = np.take(weight, target)
        loss = gather_weight * loss
        if reduction == 'mean':
            return loss.sum() / gather_weight.sum()

    if reduction == 'mean':
        loss = np.mean(loss)
    if reduction == 'sum':
        loss = np.sum(loss)

    return loss

loss = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
input = torch.randn(3, 5)
target = torch.empty(3, dtype=torch.long).random_(5)
output = loss(input, target)

my_loss = softmaxcrossentropy(input.numpy(), target.numpy())
```

>The fact that the descriptions of `system_azimuth` and `system_zenith` both refer to "slope" but not "system" might imply that they should be named `slope_azimuth` and `slope_zenith` [or `slope_tilt`].

I'm starting to agree with this. "System" is misleading, however, "slope" could be misleading too, if the pile heights vary. I've used "system" because it's really the plane that the trackers are in that's important, but in this context maybe "slope" is better?

>Suggested text: The system plane is defined by the tracker azimuth, tracker tilt and ground surface. The y-axis of the system plane is along the tracker axis in the direction of the tracker azimuth. The x-axis of the system plane is 90 degrees clockwise from the y-axis, and is parallel to the ground surface. The z-axis of the system plane is perpendicular to the plane formed by the x and y axes using the right-hand rule.

If I understand you correctly this would define a **system (or "slope") plane** that _was_ parallel to the tracker axes, whereas the point of this PR is define a system (or "slope") plane that is _not_ parallel to the tracker axes.

I wonder if the use of "system" was a poor choice. If I replace it for "slope" then for example NS trackers on an 10° mostly east facing slope would have a `slope_azimuth` mostly due east, and a `slope_tilt` of 10°. Then the corresponding "slope" plane y-axis would be mostly east (same direction as "slope" azimuth) and the "slope" plane x-axis in a RHS would be mostly south.

Here's an image of a system where the sloped plane of the trackers is facing slightly north of due east at a slope of about 10°. The trackers are aligned NS in the global coordinate system, so the global YZ plane contains the tracker axes, and the global XY plane is horizontal. Like in the example above, the "slope" plane y-axis would follow its azimuth and face mostly east, so _not_parallel to the tracker axes which are NS.

Only for the specific of case of `Linux sdist` (Python 3.7, ubuntu:14.04, gcc), I found that `conda` was downgrading Python from a cpython-based Python 3.7.12 to a PyPy-based Python 3.7.12: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4953#issuecomment-1014707402

Was able to reproduce this locally in Docker.

```shell
docker run \
    --rm \
    --env CONDA=/opt/conda \
    --entrypoint="" \
    -it lightgbm/vsts-agent:ubuntu-14.04 \
    /bin/bash

# then run the following in the container
export PATH=${CONDA}/bin:${PATH}

conda create \
    --name test-env \
    -c conda-forge \
    --override-channels \
        python=3.7

conda config --set always_yes yes --set changeps1 no
conda update -q -y conda

source activate test-env
conda install -c conda-forge --override-channels -q -y -n test-env \
    cloudpickle \
    dask \
    distributed \
    joblib \
    matplotlib \
    numpy \
    pandas \
    psutil \
    python-graphviz \
    pytest \
    scikit-learn \
    scipy
```

This resulted in the following from the `conda install` logs

```text
The following packages will be DOWNGRADED:

  python                        3.7.12-hf930737_100_cpython --> 3.7.12-0_73_pypy
  python_abi                                    3.7-2_cp37m --> 3.7-2_pypy37_pp73
  setuptools                          60.5.0-py37h89c1867_0 --> 59.8.0-py37h9c2f6ca_0
```

So I started thinking "ok, what is changing in this PR? One thing I'm proposing changing in this PR is installing `python-graphviz` with conda instead of as a separate `pip install`, to reduce the risk of conflicts.

Funny enough, it seems like that change was the cause for `conda`  choosing PyPy. Using the sample code above, I found that installing `python-graphviz` by itself, separately, does not result in `conda` choosing to downgrade from a cpython-based Python to the PyPy one.

```python
shared_len = length // num_shareds
rest = length % num_shareds
start = shared_len * index + min(index, rest)
end = start + shared_len + (index < rest)
```

Before closing out this PR, I wanted to address this comment. So the reason this exists is for the following reasons (although they might not be good enough reasons?):

 1. Conversion between what juju outputs and what pylib expects is somewhat different and the meshing of the different languages to types is somewhat problematic. Luckily it's a well trodden path in pylib and this is the same convention that's in the client facade code. So if you're hacking away at pylib it should be rather familiar to use this.
 2. We want an exact match of expected values, you don't get anything for free, so you are forced to add to the values. This has previously bitten us before, where we get some new values, unsure how to deal with them, so we either drop them or pass them on directly, either way ends up being wrong. So with this, we're saying what we want to pluck from the api response, anything else is assigned None.
 3. All values that aren't found in the api response are assigned None directly, without having to check if it exists or not. It removes a lot of if, else code.

I'm not sold on the name `_toPy` and we could change it to make more sense, but that's currently in the rest of the code base, so reuse and familiarity might be worth something?

The example for the bitwise representation above does return the correct value, however the encoding/representation isn't the one we're looking for IMHO.
It returns a string "00111111100000000000000000000000", which after encoding to bytes returns b"00111111100000000000000000000000". But each character in this bytes object represents one byte, not one bit.
We can see that by converting bytes to hex-string:
```python
>>> b"00111111100000000000000000000000".hex()
'3030313131313131313030303030303030303030303030303030303030303030'
```
0x30 is the hex code for ASCII `0` and 0x31 is `1`.
So this bytes object isn't 32 bits long, but 32 bytes.

I haven't worked with IEEE floats in network protocols yet, but some quick research showed that Modbus TCP expects the 32 bit representation that `struct.pack('>f', 1.0)` returns.

> One important thing to mention is, that the encoded IEEE floats do not have the '0x' prefix. Is that a problem?

The 0x prefix is just a hint for hex representation, it's not part of the actual number. So no, we don't have to add that.

>This assertion is made when persisting blocks that ore older than already persisted canonical blocks.

getting nitpicky, just "being older" doesn't qualify. One can write a bunch of older headers for a parallel history and it won't trigger this write. There has to be a known gap to be written to in order to trigger it.

I has led me to the following elaboration:
> The caller implicitly asserts that persisted headers are canonical here.
This assertion is made when persisting headers that are known to be part of a gap
in the canonical chain. While we do validate that the headers are valid, we can not
be 100 % sure that they will eventually lead to the expected child that fills the gap.
E.g. there is nothing preventing one from persisting an uncle as the final header to
close the gap, even if that will not be the parent of the next consecutive header.
Py-EVM makes the assumption that client code will check and prevent such a scenario.

That said, now that I wrote all this and now that we have `GapChange.GapFilled`, wouldn't it be better to simply make that extra check in Py-EVM when we fill a gap. I mean, load the existing known child (the checkpoint header) and if the ends don't match up, raise an exception. It would then be Trinitys job to handle this which probably means to just log a warning and try to close the gap another time (under the assumption that someone tried to feed us an uncle for whatever reason)

I believe there needs to be additional logic for using the schema here; I could be mistaken, but I don't believe there are tests for such cases at least.

For example, `prefer_number` above is automatically converting things that look like numbers to ints or floats, but the schema may require the field to be a string. Hence, there's the possibility of a bit of casting back and forth happening here that needs to be handled.

Similarly, if the schema defines the field to be an array of strings and the spreadsheet value is one string without the "|" character (e.g. "dog"), the string needs to be cast to an array of strings (["dog"]). On the flip side, if the schema dictates the field is a string, and someone submits a string with the "|" character, it will be converted to an array of strings via `parse_item_value`, and the validation will fail even though the string was valid.

My personal preference for handling this would be to cast all values to appropriate types and do the "type hinting" all in one location; currently, these operations are occurring in multiple places/stages of the processing, and the logic becomes more difficult to follow as to where an error arose. As the cases above demonstrate, it may not make sense to do anything with a cell value until the type from the schema is known.

Not necessarily. recycle_as_safe_continuation makes *this a continuation and adds an implicit decrement of the ref_count after the task completes its initial execute (the one that does the recycle call). So it assumes that the reference count is N+1, where N is the number of children. Or at least that the reference count is incremented N+1 times (it might not actually reach N+1 if some children are spawned and complete). With continuation passing you don't wait inside the task body for the children to complete, so you just need to make sure that the reference counting works out and the continuation is not prematurely started (unless that's ok).  Since it may not be ok, the safe version is there to prevent it.

Yeah definitely a reasonable concern -- my mental model for the backcompat story is that this has essentially identical constraints to any other `whitelist_for_serdes` thing that we have right now, with the added benefit of having the potential to remove the backcompat logic in the future (as the serialized evaluations with the old format can age out of the system as they're automatically deleted).

Even though definition-level events haven't been stored anywhere in the past, we still have had to deal with backcompat to allow objects serialized with an older version of dagster to be read by a newer version of dagster (this is the cloud case).

A sensor of the form you're talking about (reading from the stream of evaluations and doing something with them) would definitely cause additional constraints (as now an older version of dagster could be reading events written by a newer version), but in general, the changes we would make to these AutoMaterializeRule objects would simply be adding new fields, and the namedtuple serializer handles changes like this automatically.

So I definitely agree with you that there are additional constraints imposed by this method (as opposed to just storing strings directly), I just prefer a world in which we're able to definitively link a rule evaluation to the precise class that produced it, rather than trying to piece things together based off of raw strings. This lets us do things like make changes to the descriptions of these things and have those changes immediately propagate back to older evaluations, or have special highlighting for different types of rules, or put a tooltip in the UI saying "to remove this rule, do `policy.without_rule(AutoMaterializeRule.some_rule_name())`", stuff like that.

I'll admit none of those features are make or break, but I do feel like there is something conceptually nice with doing things this way

I suppose the real question is just "is making a `BaseModel` hashable condoned by Pydantic?" 

If an instance of `BaseModel` isn't hashable, then attempting to `model_construct()` it into a set or a dict key will fail, clearly and predictably. But if it does implement `__hash__`, then currently it will work fine (you wont be able to dump unless you override what it serializes to, but I'm not familiar enough with the internals to know exactly how to do that. You can do that, right?) Point being, as far as I can tell there's nothing stopping use from making it work, so the question becomes "should we bother to make it work?" It would make sense if a model with a field `set[SomeModel]` would get properly constructed into a `set` of `SomeModel`s.

If making a BaseModel hashable is something condoned (and better still if frequently done), then we should probably include the functionality. If not, I don't feel bad ignoring sets and exploding dict keys under the pretense that BaseModels are likely not going to be hashable objects.

One step is to work out what the path inside the taskcluster-web app is for viewing a task, regardless of whether it is the `taskcluster.net` deployment or any other one, and the second step is to work out the full path is, based on that subpath, and the root url. The second step is implemented in `ui` function, with the intention that everything that needs to perform this logical step, uses this function. This means that if at some point we want to change the logic, we can change it in one place only. The argument could be made that a class that implements `__nonzero__` but *not* `__bool__` is just broken on Python 3: if you want to support Python 3, you need to implement `__bool__`. It looks funny to have a class that has `__nonzero__` but not `__bool__`, and if I encountered it, the first thing I would do would be to define `__bool__`. I shouldn't need to know that a super class's `__bool__` delegates to a non-standard, Python 2-only dunder method.

It's not clear to me that we should enshrine `__nonzero__` as the canonical way for subclasses of `SecurityMap` to implement `bool()` on Python 3 in this way. 

OTOH, I can understand an argument from the practicality standpoint, if there are a large number of such subclasses that aren't actually tested on Python 3 and so would be subtlety broken: this slightly bandaids the pain. Testing them on Python 3 would reveal the problem, though, whereupon it could be fixed.

- `HttpProxyConnection()` relies on a HTTP proxy like Squid or TinyProxy. It has to accept HTTP connection and the CONNECT verb. In the context of HTTPS, the proxy will just forward the connection to the final host.

    See: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTP_tunnel#HTTP_CONNECT_method

- `SSLTunnelConnection` is different, the tunnel is established between the client and a HTTPS server. The client has to send traffic to a given end-point of the HTTPS, the traffic will end up being forwarded to the final application. So I believe it's similar to haproxy SSL passthrough for instance.

Yeah, I steered away from modeling the client as a resource because of this: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/50838525/bad-request-error-while-querying-data-from-bigquery-in-a-loop/50843371

When you construct the job config, you can either associate it with the client: `bigquery.Client(..., default_query_job_config=cfg)` 

or with the query: 

`client.query(..., job_config=cfg`)

However, if you do the former, the client is not reusable because it tries to sink query data into the same anonymous destination table in BQ. So, the config needs to be solid-level vs. resource level to support this, and consequently a BQ resource in this regime would be just a wrapper over the one-liner client instantiation, which isn't that useful

this is pretty confusing -- we do pause + save checkpoint as 2 steps:
1. first time we try to schedule a pause, we schedule a save instead. then we CACHE a pause decision.
2. then, once the save finishes, we pop the cached decision and execute it, which should be a PAUSE again, in which case we enter the `should_checkpoint=False` condition and just stop the trial and set the status to PAUSED.

In between these 2 steps, if the scheduler outputs some decision that's not PAUSED (ex: NOOP), then this thing will just hang forever. So we need to make sure that the cached PAUSE decision is not overriden by something random while the save is happening.

I allow STOP decisions to override, but unclear if this ever happens..

```python
def test_fully_exhaust_unique_booleans(faker, num_samples):
    _dummy = [faker.boolean() for _ in range(num_samples)]
    faker.unique.boolean()
    faker.unique.boolean()
    with pytest.raises(UniquenessException):
        faker.unique.boolean()
    _dummy = [faker.boolean() for _ in range(num_samples)]

def test_do_not_exhaust_booleans(faker):
    faker.unique.boolean()

def test_fully_exhaust_booleans_again(faker, num_samples):
    _dummy = [faker.boolean() for _ in range(num_samples)]
    faker.unique.boolean()
    faker.unique.boolean()
    with pytest.raises(UniquenessException):
        faker.unique.boolean()
    _dummy = [faker.boolean() for _ in range(num_samples)]
```

This line is updated as suggested. In addition, I actually make an approximation here in this line. Since the persistence current is inversely proportional to time, the integrated persistence signal over the exposure time is , where t0 is the starting time of a previous exposure since reset and t_exp is the exposure time (140.25 s). The integrated persistence signal is sensitive t0, but the timeline of observation is currently not available. So I take the persistence current at the mid-time of a exposure as the average persistence current and approximate the integrated persistence signal as ; i.e., I'm approximating the 1/t curve locally with a straight line. ![time_integrated](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/39343677/58375584-3c603900-7f24-11e9-8eac-cbdbf4a7704f.png) For more distant previous exposures (t0 > 140s), it doesn't make much difference. For the last one previous exposure, t0 (the reset time) is likely to be somewhere between 15-30s. This approximation can only provide a rough estimation for the last exposure. More precise calculation would require the whole timeline of observations.

In the future, I'd recommend creating two functions (one in marshal.py, one in unmarshal.py) that you then use in those files. They can both wrap a common function that maybe has that flag if there is no way around it. This doesn’t need to read the whole file into memory (which is
intentional in the PLY format design). The streaming code is less
susceptible to off-by-one errors in making sure that the ranges match
up, and imho easier to read:

```python
  with tf.gfile.Open(filename) as ply_file:
    for line in ply_file:
      if line.startswith('end_header'):
        break
      elif line.startswith('element vertex'):
        vert_count = int(line.split()[-1])
      elif line.startswith('element face'):
        face_count = int(line.split()[-1])
    vertex_data = [_parse_vertex(next(ply_file)) for _ in range(vert_count)]
    vertices = [datum[0] for datum in vertex_data]
    colors = [datum[1] for datum in vertex_data]
    faces = [_parse_face(next(ply_file)) for _ in range(face_count)]
  return (np.array(vertices), np.array(colors), np.array(faces))
```

No need to track `header_lines` or perform any indexing at all.

(You can swap out the `vertex_data` parsing for an all-in-one

```python
    (vertices, colors) = zip(
        *(_parse_vertex(next(ply_file)) for _ in range(vert_count))
    )
```

but personally I find that a bit less readable.)

If this is a fixed size Tuple then it would be better to not use `@to_tuple` but rather return the tuple manually (`return (x, y, z)`). The `to_tuple` decorator will always just convert a `Iterable[Something]` into a `Tuple[Something, ...]` but that doesn't convey well the intention of the API if the value is always of the same size. 

In fact, you *could* even consider creating a `NamedTuple` such as `ReleaseData` to get a stronger API. That would also eliminate the need for placeholder variables when you are only interested in one of the three values.

E.g. instead of.

```python
_, __, what_i_need = _get_release_data()
print(what_i_need)
```

You could just write

```python

release_data = _get_release_data()
print(release_data.what_i_need)
```

```
]
```

I'm pretty sure this is not "right", largely because it's not clear what the exact semantic requirements are for the `plugin.DiffResult` passed back from a provider.  Is `DetailedDiff` now required and definitive?  If so, what are the required semantics of the keys in the detailed diff?  Do they always include all subkeys?  Or never?  Are the keys canonicalized in any way (`foo.bar` vs. `["foo"]["bar"]`) - or does parsing and interpretation of those key strings have to be handled by clients?  If so, what is the exact allowed syntax and semantics clients must support?

Once all that is known, I believe this should instead operate by parsing `replaceOnChanges` as a `PropertyPath` (like with `ignoreChanges`) and then applying that path to the `DetailedDiff` properties and overwriting any changes that match with replaces.

The logic here currently supports all the "normal" cases, including normal property nesting, but likely doesn't handle a variety of unusual cases and non-standard but "legal" potential implementations of `plugin.DiffResult`.

I got here by debugging it. I noticed that we get IR frames that are somehow not answering the `is_synchronized_frame()` call (the return is false). Following the logic with Evgeni's help, we saw that the `render_loop()` checks the syncer but we didn't push to the syncer...

So we have someone pushing to a frame queue on which we do not wait, instead waiting on a syncer that gets no frames. And we know that a syncer that does not get any input has no output! So we just wait. And nothing happens.

My guess is that the concurrency work has simply made this come out, whereas before something didn't work. Or maybe it's the other way around... but this fix definitely makes more sense, and the problem goes away with it.

> What would the Enum be? There are only two variants: include or exclude.

You said in this comment https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/10923#discussion_r501942411:
> There is very little unifying about this parent class, outside of a) these are like the normal dependencies field, and b) you are going to do something magical with the values

That seems to me to imply we are going to want to do many other things with this field in the future. An enum allows for that. boolean fields are often a code smell: see https://softwareengineering.stackexchange.com/a/148058.

> such as adding yet another class you need to import from target.py, which is already gargantuan.

I think this is necessary complexity, because each element is individually cacheable. When I responded to a user in the slack yesterday who mentioned that it was a little off-putting to import so many things by mentioning how the point is to make a 100% cacheable and performant build tool, they seemed to accept that very easily.

I think if this is a concern, we could consider methods to bring elements into scope automatically, perhaps by linking classes to other classes. #10924 generalizes the `inject_for`/`infer_from` pattern with a `ConvertibleFrom` mixin, which makes a lot of the small machinery fall away, for example.

2. window mode: It first rolls the input series into a batch of subsequences, each with length=`roll_len`. Then it trains an autoencoder network on the batch of subsequences and calculate the reconstruction error. The anomaly score for each sample is a linear combinition of two parts: 1) the reconstruction error of the sample in a subsequence 2) the reconstruction error of the entire subsequence as a vector. You can use `sub_scalef` to control the weights of the 2nd part.  Note that one sample may belong to several subsequences as subsequences overlap because of rolling, and we only keep the largest anomaly score as the final score.

Sigh, I see this is the first time we’re trying to modify parts of an existing model spec we’ve loaded from disk. (For OD, we just append to the pre-trained model.)

But I think we should avoid leaking raw protobuf code into this file. My proposal is this: instead programmatically construct the architecture of the model from scratch using the existing layer-by-layer `model_spec` methods. Initialize all the weights to zero, except for the instance norm weights, for which you do what you do here. Then use existing functionality to “merge” the parameters for the other layers from the pre-trained model into the model you built programmatically. 

“But Nick, why do go through all the trouble?” I think you’ll need to do this anyway to robustly support loading from a saved model. (Since our file format only saves the parameters by name, not the layer types.) So you may as well do that now, and avoid the leaky `model_spec` abstraction. Happy to discuss in more detail if you have other ideas or context, of course!

This is a widely adopted optimization [[1](https://github.com/rwightman/pytorch-image-models/blob/a2727c1bf78ba0d7b5727f5f95e37fb7f8866b1f/timm/data/mixup.py#L26), [2](https://github.com/facebookresearch/ClassyVision/blob/8e3d7587e4102cf308ee77498fa0ff395058bc1b/classy_vision/dataset/transforms/mixup.py#L34)] which exploits the fact that the training batches are already randomized. Thus creating pairs by "folding" the batch in half instead of shuffling it is much faster. 

This introduces a limitation for supporting batches of odd size (which is usually avoided anyway as it's slower than when a power of two). There are workarounds but none of them produces as clean code as this. We could also allow odd batch sizes with the only caveat that the middle record will be "combined with itself" and thus the transform has no effect. In that case we can just throw a warning to the user.

After some more investigations, I would say that the actual implementation gives the best result: The returned thresholds [t_0, t_1, ..., t_n] separating the classes C_0, C_1, ...., C_(n+1) are defined such as
- C_0 = {x; x ≤ t_0}
- C_1 = {x; t_0 < x ≤ t_1}
- ....
- C_n+1 = {x; x ≤ t_n}

This means that the `right` parameter of `np.digitise` has to be set to `True`:
```python
for ax, thresh in zip(plt.subplots(1, 2)[1], [[1, 128], [64, 128]]): 
    regions = np.digitize(image, thresh, right=True) 
    ax.imshow(regions, cmap='Accent') 
    ax.set_title("Thresholds = {}".format(thresh)) 
plt.tight_layout()  
```
![Figure_3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3438227/66143644-d4bfa600-e607-11e9-893a-f5a420bb840f.png)

The threshold [64, 128] merges the pixels values 0 and 64 making the first circle disappear!
With the thresholds set to [1, 128] the values 64 and 128 are considered as one class and no circle are lost!

If the number of classes is set to 4, both implementations return [0, 64, 128] and all the circles are separated!

```
        def equalize(splits: List[Dataset[T]]) -> List[Dataset[T]]:
            if not equal or len(splits) == 0:
                return splits

            target_size = sum([s.count() for s in splits]) / len(splits) 
            left_over = None

            # first round: trim the large split
            for i, s in enumerate(splits):
                if s.count() <= target_size:
                    continue                
                s, remainder = s.split_at_indices([target_size])
                splits[i] = s
                left_over = left_over.union(remainder) if left_over else remainder

            if not left_over or left_over.count() == 0:
                return

            # second round: fill the small split
            for i, s in enumerate(splits):
                if s.count() == target_size:
                    continue                
                required = target_size - s.count()
                addition, left_over = left_over.split_at_indices([required])
                splits[i] = s.union(addition)
            
            return splits
```

Deleted this comment initially - adding it back for posterity though. I ran into a couple sections of code that I thought would break the approach i propose and wanted to take some time to understand the current implementation before considering an api change in addition

Original message:

I've been working through implementing this and running into some complexities...

Right now, all time window partitions except hourly format the partition keys as `Y-m-d` but the addition of hour and minute offsets means we need to switch to `Y-m-d-h-m` format. seems like nbd, but the time window partitions expect the `start` to be formatted in the same way as the partition keys. so that means every partition with a `start` in format `Y-m-d` (which is what all of our testing and docs use) no longer works. 

One option is that we could make it so time window partitions can handle a `start` that's a different format than what the keys will be.

But this has made me think even more about the [design question](https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/pull/7125#pullrequestreview-914691148) i had in this PR. I'm starting to lean in the direction that we should support users passing the start in `Y-m-d-h-m` format and base the windows off of that. 

Example:

```
@daily_partitioned_config(start="2022-2-18-02:15")
# creates partitions (2022-2-18-02:15, 2022-2-19-02:15), (2022-2-19-02:15, 2022-2-20-02:15) ...

@weekly_partitioned_config(start="2022-2-18-03:10")
# creates partitions (2022-2-18-03:10, 2022-2-25-03:10), (2022-2-25-03:10, 2022-3-04-03:10)...

@daily_partitioned_config(start="2022-2-16", fmt="%Y-%m-%d")
# creates partitions (2022-2-16, 2022-2-17), (2022-2-17, 2022-2-18)...
```
This reads cleaner to me and someone reading the partition will know exactly when the first partition starts, rather than needing to know implementations details like that a weekly partition actually begins the sunday after the start unless otherwise specified. 

It also reduces collisions if a user specifies a datetime as start. What is the expected behavior in a situation like this?
`@daily_partitioned_config(start=datetime(2022, 2, 18, 2, 15), minute_offset=30, hour_offset=5)`


To reduce the number of breaking changes, we could make the default string format for `start` `Y-m-d` and document how to change the format to support hour/minute/day offsets. But this would still introduce breaking changes for weekly and monthly partitions because the start date of the partition would change to match the use specified time

I think that the problem is you're trying to fit all the pandas storage-format-specific methods into a base class. We should not do that. The modin architecture shouldn't be limited by a single type of execution backend (the one that supports arbitrary lambdas/lazy execution/so on), the base class has to be general enough to be implemented by a various amount of execution systems, not only the one that's ray+pandas like.

> I think we can consider that partitions are an internal implementation detail, and not require for each implementation

The experience with integrating Omnisci into Modin shows, that even non-partition-based execution may exploit the partition concept by limiting the total amount of partitions to 1 and so being able to smoothly integrate into a partitioned-based execution system (Modin).

> So inheritance for OmniSci partition is removed.

Doing that makes a risk to end-up with monolith architecture where none of the layers can be replaced with each other, instead of the original concept, where it's implied that we can seamlessly replace one layer with another and everything should work properly (maybe not optimal, but properly). The refactoring activities should move us toward the goal of layers' independency, not throwing us back.

So, what's my suggestion? My suggestion is to carefully think about the base partition API, exclude all of the execution-specific methods, discuss the API with our consortium (apparently modin-core members), and define a decent base API that would be suitable for any Modin's implementation.

> It requires significant code refactoring. For example, `ExternalNetifMulticastAddress` will be put to a `LinkedList<ExternalNetifMulticastAddress>` but `ExternalNetifMulticastAddress.GetNext()` returns a NetifMulticastAddress, which violates the protocol of LinkedList.

What I had in mind  was not to create a new actual linked-list of `LinkedList<ExternalNetifMulticastAddress>`. I agree that would not be good and would require a lot of changes.

The idea was that we really have one actual linked-list in memory which is a list of all `NetifMulticastAddress` objects. We define a new sub-class `ExtNetifMulticastAdderss` where we just overload the `GetNext()` to go through the same linked-list but skip over all entries that are not external (i.e., make it look like as if the external addresses are in a linked-list of their own).

 But considering how to implement this, I realized it may not be as easy as I initially thought - main thing is that we cannot tell in `(Ext)NetifMulticastAdderss` class itself whether an entry is external or not. For this, we need to have have access to `Netif` class - which we can get if we make `ExtNetifMulticastAddress` be an `InstanceLocatorInit` (all doable  but requires more changes).

https://github.com/jabesq/pyatmo/blob/9c1f05f5ea40faaaf2ef2a2a67a8aa4793d91d83/src/pyatmo/auth.py#L173-L177

In this case the m_Cache is just a psuedo random number generator that is computing ((((X0*A+X1)*A+X2)*A+X3)*A+X4%2^n using a LCG (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linear_congruential_generator) where the incoming addresses are providing the randomness in the form of c to the classic formula (A*X+c) mod 2^n.   

The magic of the number is just to spread the bits picked in this case by randomly bashing keys on the keyboard so that we are less likely to have collisions because memory addressing  will have very similar address keys.    The number is expected to just keep overflowing due to 2^n mod math.  The number using for spreading doesn't need to be prime in the field, it just has to have sufficient entropy that we are unlikely to get a sequence where A*X1+X2 = A*X2+X1.  

The good news is about this particular algorithm is collisions don't actually matter.  Say we have 3 overloads which because of the way the user is hitting them with arguments are forming 5 different hits in the table.  That will form 5 hash codes in our m_Cache.  Each time one is called and it doesn't match the previous call, we cache the method and random code assigned.  If the hash in m_Cache does match we test the cached method to see if it is a match.   If two of the methods have a collision of their argument cache, we pay a cache miss on the test of the next method call (just loss of speed), but if we have a cache hit we get to realize the last resolution address skipping deciding which of the 3 overloads to use.  So we get cost=1 on a hit, cost=3 on a miss (we find no match on the hash), and cost=4 on a collision (one extra test).  Assuming  typical programming with 80% hits, our speed is .8*1+0.2*3.  If one of the 5 has collision with 10% usage it would be 0.7*1+0.2*3+0.1*4.  So given that there is no cause for alarm if we get a collision, and the chance of getting a collision given the entropy of the inputs is staggeringly low this is a pretty safe algorithm for deciding is the user hitting the same overload again and again.

I will add the reference to this being a LCG to make it more clear.  (I could have gone and written a program to help chose the optimal magic number given a set of addresses, but that would have been way more work that randomly generating a number by blindly typing on the keyboard)   Maybe I should have named it m_Hash?

That is how I did it in my initial implementation, but the problem lies in the `_allocate_size` function.

When multiple threads are building trees and one of them has to resize the memory (with `realloc`), it invalidates all pointers  held by other threads here: https://github.com/spotify/annoy/blob/master/src/annoylib.h#L1199
This leads to a segmentation fault. To guard against this, each thread would have to copy the children before using them. Additionally, all `_allocate_size` calls would have to be guarded by an exclusive mutex and all `_get` calls with a shared mutex (because we would have to ensure `_get` does not return an invalid pointer). This resulted in a significant slow-down due to mutex contention and increase in memory usage due to copying.

Sooo, this one is tricky. This is asking for `Files`, which would be symlink oblivious. So I think that you'd need to actually walk the symlink in this case to determine whether the destination was a file.

But also: if the destination is a `Directory`, then you'd need to recurse with a new relative path.

----

I see two options for these cases (where a `DigestTrie` needs to actually "act" like a filesystem):
1. adding another implementation of `DigestTrie::walk` that is symlink oblivious (i.e. will never return a symlink, and instead will walk within the destination of the symlink)
2. Adding methods to `VFS` to implement walking, and/or using the equivalent of `vfs.expand(PathGlobs(["**"]))`, and using them when you want to be symlink oblivious.

The former might be easier? It would be lightly redundant with path globs expansion, but on the other hand, pretending that something is POSIX when it isn't doesn't do you many favors.

This actually doesn't affect command-line parsing in 'real' applications, which use `KVArgParseConfigLoader` below. `KVArgParseConfig` works like this:

1. build an argparse Parser to handle aliases and flags
2. call `parse_known_args` to get alias and flag config,
3. construct one of these `KeyValueConfigLoader`s to handle parsing the remaining *unrecognized* arguments, which should all be arbitrary `--Class.trait=foo` (or unrecognized aliases that should raise).

The result is that these `KeyValueConfigLoader` objects are *only* used to parse full `--Class.trait` args, never aliases or flags. In changing the implementation here to use argparse (mainly to get consistent multiply-specified and `=` vs space handling), I lost the previous support for aliases, which was never used, since these were by definition used only on non-alias/flag options. Rather than complicate things further by reimplementing alias support where it will never be used, I opted to make the lack of support in thus sub-parser explicit.

@vfdev-5 no, I don't have a reference for this, but I have a few comments:
1. I think ernestums implementation and ours are not really comparable when it comes to the parameters, since ernestum uses `scipy.ndimage.interpolation.map_coordinates`  which expects as an identity sampling grid this `np.meshgrid(np.arange(shape[0]), np.arange(shape[1]), np.arange(shape[2]))`, so values lie between 0 and H or W.  In our implementation we use `vision.torchvision.transforms.functional_tensor._apply_grid_transform` which expects as an identity grid this `[torch.linspace((-s + 1) / s, (s - 1) / s, s) for s in size]`, so values lie between -1:1 for any given image independant of the shape. Anyways, I think the smoothing shouldn't be affected by this
2. I looked into imgaugs Elastic Transformation and they calculate the kernel_size based on the sigma like this:
`def _compute_gaussian_blur_ksize(sigma):
    if sigma < 3.0:
        ksize = 3.3 * sigma  # 99% of weight
    elif sigma < 5.0:
        ksize = 2.9 * sigma  # 97% of weight
    else:
        ksize = 2.6 * sigma  # 95% of weight

    # we use 5x5 here as the minimum size as that simplifies
    # comparisons with gaussian_filter() in the tests
    # TODO reduce this to 3x3
    ksize = int(max(ksize, 5))
    return ksize` 
I don't think that this is necessary for our implementation.
3. I ran our implementation as is `elastic_transform(img,alpha=[40,40],sigma=[4,4], random_state=0)` and with the kernel size changed to `int(8 * sigma + 1)` and stored the results in the red channel of the as is implementation in the blue channel and the one with the bigger kernel size in the red channel and the results look identical:
![red_8_blue_4](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22618181/152697574-03a1b815-0a49-4be6-9bb1-36758fd99045.PNG)
 
 So all in all, I think we can change it to `int(8 * sigma + 1)` for consistency with ernestums implementation. Sorry for the late reply and thanks for all your work :)

Having the Gitlab script copy sort.py into here, and then deleting it, makes the setup very tightly coupled: you can *only* run this script as part of the Gitlab script. If you run it on its own, manually, it will complain that sort.py isn't there. If you provide it, it will run once, but delete it, so you can't run it again to test something else without providing a new copy of sort.py.

If the test fails, sort.py sticks around, untracked and un-cleaned-up.

As we saw in previous optimizations (for example #6821), removing clone in favour of moving the copy on the first multiplication has speed benefits. Below I benchmark the following two alternatives for various inputs (images and labels) to showcase this:

```python
def withclone(inpt, lam=0.5):
    output = inpt.clone()
    return output.roll(1, 0).mul_(1.0 - lam).add_(output.mul_(lam))


def withoutclone(inpt, lam=0.5):
    return inpt.roll(1, 0).mul_(1.0 - lam).add_(inpt.mul(lam))
```

Below we benchmark the 2 above methods alone:

```
[-------------- Mixing cpu torch.float32 --------------]
                         |  withclone   |  withoutclone 
1 threads: ---------------------------------------------
      (16, 3, 400, 400)  |    41200     |      15200    
      (16, 1000)         |       22     |         20    
6 threads: ---------------------------------------------
      (16, 3, 400, 400)  |    17700     |      15400    
      (16, 1000)         |       22     |         20    

Times are in microseconds (us).

[------------- Mixing cuda torch.float32 --------------]
                         |  withclone   |  withoutclone 
1 threads: ---------------------------------------------
      (16, 3, 400, 400)  |    274.9     |       226     
      (16, 1000)         |     33.2     |        26     
6 threads: ---------------------------------------------
      (16, 3, 400, 400)  |    275.6     |       226     
      (16, 1000)         |     33.0     |        26     

Times are in microseconds (us).
```

I disagree that TCP can handle `mLength` being increased without special handling. Depending on the amount by which `mLength` is increased, TCP may need to send out additional data immediately. As a concrete example, some data in the send buffer may not be sent out immediately due to "silly window avoidance" but a call to `otTcpActiveSendByExtension` causes the threshold to be reached and data to be sent out.

Regarding public API, we can also use same model of defining a common `struct` type that covers different key types to avoid the need to have different flavors of same API.

Here is my suggestion:
```c
/**
 * This type represents a crypto key.
 *
 */
typedef struct otCryptoKey
{
    const uint8_t *mKey;      ///< Pointer to the buffer containing key. NULL indicates to use `mKeyRef`.
    uint16_t       mKeyLength ///< The key length in bytes (applicable when `mKey` is not NULL).
    uint32_t       mKeyRef;   ///< The PSA key ref (requires `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_PSA_CRYPTO_ENABLE` and `mKey` to be NULL).
} otCryptoKey;
```

And then have a single API that gets a `otCryptoKey` as input:
```c
void otCryptoHmacSha256(const otCryptoKey *aKey,
                        const uint8_t *     aBuf,
                        uint16_t            aBufLength,
                        otCryptoSha256Hash *aHash);
```

I used a simple struct for `otCryptoKey` and used `mKey` being set to NULL to indicate the "type" of key (whether the key bytes are directly given or if to use the `mKeyRef` value). We can consider using `union` or other data models, but I think such `struct` is simple and easy to use.

I don't agree that it is the same. If you use `run_in_transaction`, aborted transactions will automatically be retried by the client library. In this implementation any aborted transaction will be propagated as an error to the client application. That means that if you use `execute` in autocommit mode, statements will automatically be retried if the underlying transaction is aborted, while if you use `execute_many` in autocommit mode, the aborted error will be propagated to the client application. I don't think that is a very good API.
The other option would be to use the checksum retry logic for autocommit as well, but that seems like over-complicating things to me.
